q: Which of the following is the best definition of the digestive system?
options: The alimentary canal alone, responsible for food breakdown
The alimentary canal and accessory digestive organs as an integrated system for ingestion, digestion, and absorption
The stomach and intestines working together to absorb nutrients
The set of organs that produce digestive enzymes and hormones
The gastrointestinal tract plus the circulatory system
explain: The digestive system is defined as the alimentary canal AND accessory digestive organs (e.g., liver, pancreas, salivary glands) working as an integrated system. The alimentary canal alone is not the complete digestive system, and the circulatory system is not part of it.

q: Segmentation in the intestine is best classified as which type of digestion?
options: Chemical digestion, because it involves enzyme secretion
Mechanical digestion, because it physically mixes and breaks down food
Both mechanical and chemical digestion simultaneously
Neither; segmentation is a form of absorption
Chemical digestion, because it requires energy from ATP
explain: Segmentation is rhythmic local constriction of the intestinal wall that physically mixes and breaks food particles — this is mechanical digestion. No enzymes are involved in the segmentation movement itself, so it is not chemical digestion.

q: Which three monosaccharides are produced by carbohydrate digestion and absorbed into the bloodstream?
options: Glucose, sucrose, and galactose
Fructose, maltose, and glucose
Glucose, fructose, and galactose
Galactose, ribose, and glucose
Sucrose, fructose, and lactose
explain: Carbohydrates are broken down to monosaccharides — specifically glucose, fructose, and galactose — which are then absorbed into the bloodstream. Sucrose, maltose, and lactose are disaccharides that must be further broken down; ribose is a pentose not produced from dietary polysaccharide digestion.

q: Dietary fats are mainly which type of molecule, and what are their digestion end-products absorbed by the intestine?
options: Phospholipids; broken down into fatty acids and glycerophosphate
Cholesterol esters; broken down into free cholesterol and fatty acids
Triglycerides; broken down into glycerol and free fatty acids
Triglycerides; broken down into glucose and fatty acids
Sterols; broken down into bile acids and fatty acids
explain: Dietary fat consists mainly of triglycerides. These are hydrolysed into glycerol and free fatty acids, which are then absorbed by the intestine. Phospholipids and cholesterol esters are not the primary dietary fat form, and glucose is not a product of fat digestion.

q: What is the primary function of the stomach in the GI tract?
options: Absorption of all major food products into the blood
Final chemical digestion of all carbohydrates
Receiving, mechanically mixing food with secretions, and partial digestion — with no absorption
Secretion of bile to emulsify fats
Storage and concentration of undigested waste for defecation
explain: The stomach acts as a reservoir, mechanically mixes food with saliva and gastric secretions, and performs only partial digestion. Critically, no absorption of food products occurs in the stomach. Bile is produced by the liver, and final absorption and waste storage are functions of other regions.

q: Which region of the GI tract is primarily responsible for the absorption of ALL food products?
options: Stomach
Esophagus
Large intestine
Small intestine
Pharynx
explain: Absorption of all food products (amino acids, monosaccharides, fatty acids, etc.) occurs in the small intestine (midgut). The stomach performs no significant absorption. The large intestine absorbs water, ions, and some vitamins but not the major food products.

q: Bile is produced by which organ and stored by which organ?
options: Produced by the gallbladder; stored by the pancreas
Produced by the pancreas; stored by the liver
Produced by the liver; stored by the gallbladder
Produced by the gallbladder; stored by the liver
Produced and stored by the liver
explain: Bile is produced (secreted) by the liver and stored and concentrated between meals by the gallbladder. The pancreas produces digestive enzymes and bicarbonate, not bile. Confusing the site of production with the site of storage is a common error.

q: What exocrine secretions does the stomach produce, and what is the function of HCl specifically?
options: Amylase, lipase, and bicarbonate; HCl digests proteins
HCl, pepsin, and mucus; HCl solubilises food particles and kills microbes
Bile salts, bicarbonate, and pepsin; HCl neutralises alkaline food
HCl, amylase, and mucus; HCl activates amylase
Pepsin, lipase, and bicarbonate; HCl emulsifies fat
explain: The stomach secretes HCl, pepsin (a protein-digesting enzyme), and mucus. HCl specifically solubilises food particles and kills microbes. Amylase is a salivary/pancreatic enzyme; bile is from the liver; bicarbonate comes from the pancreas and liver.

q: Which organ secretes bicarbonate into the small intestine primarily to neutralise HCl arriving from the stomach?
options: Liver only
Gallbladder
Stomach
Pancreas only
Both the pancreas and liver secrete bicarbonate for this purpose
explain: According to the lecture table, both the pancreas and the liver secrete bicarbonate to neutralise HCl entering the small intestine from the stomach. The gallbladder only stores bile; the stomach secretes HCl itself.

q: What are the four microscopic layers of the GI tract wall, listed from innermost (lumen) to outermost?
options: Serosa, muscularis, submucosa, mucosa
Mucosa, muscularis, submucosa, serosa
Mucosa, submucosa, muscularis, serosa
Submucosa, mucosa, serosa, muscularis
Epithelium, lamina propria, submucosa, serosa
explain: From the lumen outward, the four layers are: mucosa (innermost), submucosa, muscularis, and serosa (outermost). The muscularis comes after the submucosa, not before it. The epithelium and lamina propria are sub-components of the mucosa, not separate wall layers.

q: Where is the myenteric plexus (plexus of Auerbach) located in the GI wall?
options: Within the mucosa, between the epithelium and lamina propria
Within the submucosa
Between the longitudinal and circular muscle layers of the muscularis
External to the serosa
Within the lamina propria of the mucosa
explain: The myenteric plexus (Auerbach) lies between the longitudinal and circular muscle layers of the muscularis and primarily controls GI motility. The submucosal plexus (Meissner) is in the submucosa and primarily controls secretion. These two plexuses are frequently swapped.

q: The submucosal plexus (plexus of Meissner) is located in which layer of the GI wall?
options: Between the two muscle layers of the muscularis
Within the mucosa
In the submucosa
In the serosa
Between the mucosa and submucosa only
explain: The submucosal plexus (Meissner) is located within the submucosa and primarily controls glandular secretion and mucosal movement. The myenteric plexus (Auerbach) sits between the muscle layers and controls motility. Exam questions often test this distinction.

q: Peristalsis differs from segmentation in that peristalsis:
options: Involves only circular muscle and does not produce net propulsion
Involves both longitudinal and circular muscles and primarily propels food aborally
Occurs exclusively in the large intestine
Is initiated by chemical stimulants in the lumen rather than distention
Involves only longitudinal muscle and is asynchronous
explain: Peristalsis is a travelling wave of constriction caused by coordinated action of BOTH longitudinal and circular smooth muscles, moving food aborally (away from the mouth). Segmentation, by contrast, involves only circular muscle and is asynchronous — its primary function is mixing, not net propulsion.

q: Which statement about segmentation is correct?
options: It involves both longitudinal and circular muscles contracting in sequence
Its primary function is to propel food aborally
It is a travelling wave that moves along the intestine
It involves asynchronous contractions of circular muscle only, with no longitudinal muscle
It occurs exclusively in the esophagus
explain: Segmentation is an asynchronous, non-travelling constriction of the gut tube that involves ONLY the circular muscle — the longitudinal muscle is NOT involved. Its primary purpose is to mix and break down food particles, not to propel them. It occurs mainly in the small and large intestines.

q: What initiates peristalsis in the GI tract?
options: Chemical composition of the chyme acting on chemoreceptors
Hormonal signals from the pancreas
Distention (stretching) of the gut wall
Sympathetic nerve stimulation
Temperature changes within the lumen
explain: According to the lecture, peristalsis is initiated mainly by distention (stretching) of the gut wall. While chemoreceptors and hormones can modulate GI motility, the primary trigger for peristalsis is mechanical stretching from luminal contents. Sympathetic stimulation is inhibitory to motility.

q: In which sites does peristalsis occur?
options: Only in the esophagus
Esophagus, distal stomach, small intestine, and large intestine
Small intestine and large intestine only
Stomach and small intestine only
Esophagus and small intestine only
explain: The lecture states peristalsis occurs in the esophagus, the distal stomach, the small intestine, and the large intestine. Segmentation (not peristalsis) is confined to the small and large intestines. The esophagus uses peristalsis exclusively for propulsion.

q: Which of the following is NOT a function of GI tract movement?
options: Translocation of food from one location to another
Mechanical breakdown of food particles
Mixing food to renew contact with secreting and absorbing surfaces
Chemical digestion of proteins by proteolytic enzymes
All of the above are functions of GI movement
explain: The three functions of GI movement are translocation, mechanical breakdown, and mixing for renewal of surface contact. Chemical digestion by enzymes is a function of secretory glands, not of the movement itself. Movement and chemical digestion are distinct processes.

q: What does intrinsic control of GI motility involve?
options: Sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems acting on gut smooth muscle
GI peptide hormones secreted in response to luminal chemical stimulants
The enteric nervous system, modulated by gut wall stretching and chyme composition
Central nervous system commands transmitted via the vagus nerve only
Hormonal signals from the pancreas and liver
explain: Intrinsic control is mediated by the enteric nervous system (ENS) and is triggered by stretching of the GI tract wall and by substances in the chyme. GI hormones and the autonomic nervous system represent extrinsic control. The vagus nerve is a component of extrinsic parasympathetic control, not intrinsic.

q: How do the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems differ in their effects on GI motility?
options: Both are excitatory; they differ only in speed of action
Sympathetic is excitatory; parasympathetic is inhibitory
Sympathetic is inhibitory; parasympathetic is excitatory
Both are inhibitory under resting conditions
Sympathetic controls secretion; parasympathetic controls motility only
explain: The sympathetic nervous system is inhibitory to GI motility (reducing it), while the parasympathetic nervous system is excitatory (increasing motility and secretion). This is a classic exam distinction — the "fight or flight" sympathetic response slows digestion, while the "rest and digest" parasympathetic response promotes it.

q: The enteric nervous system (ENS) is sometimes called the 'little brain in the gut' because it:
options: Is directly controlled by the cerebral cortex for all digestive reflexes
Contains approximately 100 million neurons and operates as an independent integrative system
Contains more neurons than the human brain
Is formed entirely of sensory neurons that relay signals to the CNS
Consists only of the myenteric plexus
explain: The ENS contains about 100 million neurons — more than a mouse brain — and is organised as an independent integrative system capable of coordinating GI reflexes without CNS input. It contains more neurons than a mouse brain, not the human brain (which has ~86 billion neurons). It consists of both myenteric and submucosal plexuses.

q: Which of the following correctly lists the three types of neurons found in the enteric nervous system?
options: Sensory neurons, motor neurons, and glial cells
Mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, and thermoreceptors
Sensory neurons, interneurons, and motor neurons
Preganglionic, postganglionic, and interneurons
Adrenergic, cholinergic, and serotonergic neurons
explain: The ENS contains sensory neurons (detecting mechanical, chemical, and thermal stimuli), interneurons (coordinating reflexes and motor programs), and motor neurons (excitatory or inhibitory, targeting muscle, secretory epithelium, endocrine cells, and blood vessels). Mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, and thermoreceptors are subtypes of sensory neurons, not the three broad neuron types.

q: The ENS myenteric plexus consists of two named components. What is a 'plexus' in this context?
options: A gland that secretes digestive enzymes
A layer of smooth muscle
A network of neurons
A bundle of blood vessels
A mucous membrane lining
explain: According to the lecture, a plexus is defined as 'a network of neurons.' Both the myenteric plexus (between muscle layers) and the submucosal plexus (in submucosa) are neural networks, not glands, muscles, or blood vessels.

q: In Hirschsprung's disease, which segment of the large intestine is abnormal, and what is the clinical consequence?
options: The proximal (normal) colon lacks nerve cells and becomes narrow
Nerve cells fail to form in part or all of the large intestine, causing the affected segment to block waste and the normal colon proximal to it to swell
Nerves form in excess, causing uncontrolled propulsion and diarrhoea
The small intestine loses its nerve supply, blocking absorption
The rectum hypertrophies and prevents defecation
explain: In Hirschsprung's disease, nerves fail to form in the affected (aganglionic) segment of the large intestine, so waste cannot pass through it. The normal colon proximal to the blockage swells with accumulated stool. It is the normal segment that dilates — not the diseased segment.

q: Saliva from the salivary glands serves which two primary roles in the mouth?
options: Solubilises proteins and activates bile production
Lubricates food (mechanical aid) and contains amylase for chemical digestion of starch
Provides HCl for disinfection and mucus for lubrication
Begins fat emulsification and lubricates the esophagus
Contains pepsin for protein digestion and water to dissolve minerals
explain: Saliva serves two roles: it lubricates food to aid mechanical digestion, and it contains salivary amylase which begins chemical digestion of polysaccharides (starch). HCl and pepsin are gastric, not salivary, secretions. Bile emulsifies fat in the small intestine, not the mouth.

q: What is the primary function of the large intestine (hindgut)?
options: Absorption of all food macronutrients
Secretion of digestive enzymes and bicarbonate
Accumulation and dehydration of digestion waste; absorption of water, ions, and some vitamins
Mechanical shaking and mixing of food for enzymatic digestion
Propulsion of food by peristalsis into the small intestine
explain: The large intestine (hindgut/colon) accumulates and dehydrates digestion waste and absorbs water, ions, and some vitamins. Macro-nutrient absorption is the function of the small intestine. The large intestine does not secrete digestive enzymes; this is mainly a pancreatic and small intestinal function.

q: A patient's colon lacks the myenteric plexus in a segment. Which motility type would be most directly impaired in that segment?
options: Chemical digestion, because the plexus releases enzymes
Bile secretion into the intestine
Peristalsis and segmentation, because both depend on ENS coordination of smooth muscle
Only absorption, because the plexus controls mucosal transport
Salivary secretion, because the ENS controls all secretions
explain: The myenteric plexus (Auerbach) coordinates smooth muscle activity for both peristalsis and segmentation. Without it, the smooth muscle of the affected segment cannot receive the neural signals needed for coordinated motility. Chemical digestion uses enzymes from glands, not the plexus; absorption and salivation are not directly controlled by the myenteric plexus.

q: Why does food move through the GI tract even in the absence of gravity, as seen in space?
options: Osmotic pressure gradients drive fluid and food toward the large intestine
Enzymatic activity creates pressure that pushes food along
GI motility is driven entirely by smooth muscle contractions, independent of gravity
Bile secretion creates a pressure gradient that propels food distally
The negative pressure of the thorax draws food into the gut
explain: The lecture specifically notes that food moves down the GI tract in the absence of gravity (e.g., in space), demonstrating that GI mobility is entirely dependent on muscle-driven motility — peristalsis and segmentation — not gravity. Osmotic pressure, enzymatic activity, and bile flow do not drive food translocation.

q: Which of the following correctly compares peristalsis and segmentation?
options: Peristalsis: circular muscle only, mixing; Segmentation: both muscles, propulsion
Peristalsis: both longitudinal and circular, propulsion, aboral; Segmentation: circular only, mixing, non-travelling
Peristalsis: longitudinal only, propulsion; Segmentation: both muscles, mixing
Peristalsis: non-travelling, mixing; Segmentation: travelling, propulsion
Peristalsis and segmentation both use both muscle layers and have the same primary function
explain: Peristalsis uses both longitudinal and circular smooth muscles to produce a travelling wave that propels food aborally. Segmentation uses only circular muscle in an asynchronous, non-travelling pattern whose primary function is mixing and mechanical breakdown. These are the definitive distinctions tested in exams.

q: The pancreas is classified as an accessory digestive organ. Which of the following statements about it is correct according to this lecture?
options: It produces bile salts to emulsify fats
It secretes HCl and pepsin for protein digestion
It secretes hydrolytic enzymes and bicarbonate; it also has nondigestive endocrine functions
It stores and concentrates bile between meals
It is part of the hindgut and absorbs vitamins
explain: The pancreas secretes hydrolytic enzymes (to digest carbohydrates, fats, proteins, and nucleic acids) and bicarbonate (to neutralise gastric acid in the small intestine). The lecture also notes it has nondigestive endocrine functions (e.g., insulin secretion). Bile salts are from the liver; HCl and pepsin are from the stomach; bile storage is the gallbladder.

q: A student claims the headgut performs absorption of amino acids. Based on the lecture, this is incorrect because:
options: The headgut absorbs fats, not amino acids
The headgut only receives and begins processing food; no absorption occurs there
Amino acid absorption requires bile from the liver, which is only available in the hindgut
The headgut lacks a lumen through which nutrients could pass
Amino acids are absorbed only by the large intestine
explain: The headgut (mouth and pharynx) is responsible only for food intake and primary mechanical processing — chewing, lubrication, and initiation of starch digestion. No absorption occurs in the headgut. Absorption of amino acids (and all other food products) is a function of the small intestine (midgut).
